blurb: Little teardrop-shaped beads, pointed at the tip and striped with translucent 'windows', strung along trailing stems. The daintier cousin of String of Pearls.

care.light: Bright, indirect light with a little gentle sun keeps the beads plump and close-spaced; harsh sun shrivels them and deep shade makes the strands sparse and stretched.
care.water: Soak-and-dry — water when the beads start to look a touch soft, then let it dry. The shallow roots rot quickly if kept wet.
care.soil: A gritty, fast-draining mix; a wide shallow pot suits the shallow roots.
care.dormancy: Grows in the milder months and eases off in extreme heat; keep it on the dry side then.
care.feeding: A dilute feed once or twice in the growing season.
care.potting: Lovely trailing from a raised pot; lay stray strands on the soil and they root to fill in.
care.propagation: Very easy — coil a strand onto dry mix and the nodes root.
care.bloom: Tiny pale-yellowish brush-like flowers, clustered on short stalks from late summer into winter.
care.troubles: Overwatering and rot are the main risks, plus the occasional aphid or mealybug.
care.toxicity: Like other senecios, toxic to cats and dogs if eaten — the fleshy leaves are poisonous, so keep it out of reach.

sources.title: Curio citriformis (String of Tears) — LLIFLE Encyclopedia of Succulents
Groundsel (Senecio spp.) — ASPCA Toxic and Non-Toxic Plants
